applicantowner_information.applicant_address: 215 North Main Street, Suite 402, Greenville, SC 29601

property_information.special_district: Downtown Design Overlay District (DDOD) – West End Subdistrict

description_of_request.description_of_request:
This application is for a Certificate of Appropriateness for the redevelopment of an existing one-story commercial structure at 312 River Street into a three-story mixed-use building with ground-floor retail and two levels of office space above. The project site is located within the Downtown Design Overlay District (DDOD), West End Subdistrict, and is currently occupied by a vacant masonry structure dating from the late 1960s that has been substantially altered over time and has limited architectural or historic significance.

SCOPE OF WORK
1. Demolition and Site Preparation
- Carefully demolish the existing single-story commercial building while salvaging any reusable brick and metal elements for potential incorporation into site walls and interior features.
- Maintain the existing building footprint along River Street to preserve the established streetwall, with a slight step-back at the upper levels to reduce perceived massing along the public realm.
- Protect existing street trees and utilities along River Street, coordinating with the City’s Public Works Department to ensure compliance with tree protection and right-of-way regulations.

2. New Building Construction
- Construct a three-story mixed-use building of approximately 16,800 gross square feet.
- Ground Floor (approx. 5,600 SF): Two tenant spaces intended for neighborhood-serving retail or restaurant uses, with primary entrances directly on River Street and secondary access from the rear parking area.
- Second and Third Floors (approx. 11,200 SF total): Flexible office suites designed to attract small professional firms (e.g., design studios, tech startups, and local service providers).
- Provide a recessed main lobby entrance for the upper floors at the north end of the façade, clearly distinguished architecturally from the retail entries.

3. Architectural Design and Materials
- Primary Façade (River Street):
  - Material palette includes red and brown modular brick with a sandblasted finish, fiber-cement panel accents at the upper stories, and aluminum-clad wood storefront systems at the ground level.
  - The design intentionally references the historic warehouse and mill character of the West End through the use of vertically proportioned windows, expressed brick piers, and a strong cornice line at the third floor.
  - The ground-floor storefronts will feature large, clear-glass display windows (low-iron insulated glass) with transoms above, and a brick bulkhead approximately 18–24 inches in height for durability along the sidewalk.
  - Storefront framing will be dark bronze anodized aluminum with recessed entry doors to provide weather protection and comply with accessibility requirements.

- Upper Floors:
  - The second and third floors are set back approximately 4 feet from the ground-floor façade line, creating a modest terrace condition on the River Street side that helps to reduce the building’s perceived height from the sidewalk.
  - Windows will be vertically oriented aluminum-clad casement and fixed units with divided-light patterns that echo the rhythm of traditional industrial sash windows found in nearby historic structures.
  - A brick parapet with a precast concrete cap will screen rooftop mechanical units and provide a strong, finished edge to the building silhouette.

- Rear and Side Elevations:
  - Rear façade materials will be primarily brick with fiber-cement panels at stair towers and service areas, maintaining a clean, orderly appearance toward the parking lot and adjacent properties.
  - Service doors and utility areas will be consolidated and screened with metal and wood fencing, using a dark, recessive color palette to minimize visual impact.

4. Site Design, Access, and Parking
- Vehicular access to the site will be provided from the existing shared drive aisle at the rear of the property, accessed off River Street via a curb cut that will remain in its current location.
- On-site parking will be limited to 14 spaces located behind the building, including:
  - 1 ADA-accessible parking space with an accessible route to the building entrance.
  - 2 spaces equipped with EV-ready conduit to support future installation of electric vehicle charging stations.
- Bicycle parking will be provided in the form of 6 inverted-U racks near the main building entry, visible from River Street and accessible from the sidewalk.
- Pedestrian connectivity will be enhanced by:
  - New, wider concrete sidewalks (minimum 8 feet clear) along the River Street frontage.
  - A clearly marked pedestrian path from the rear parking area to the building entrances.
  - Decorative concrete pavers at the primary building entrance and at the corners of the site to visually differentiate pedestrian zones.

5. Landscaping and Streetscape Improvements
- The landscape design is intended to complement the existing West End streetscape standards while introducing additional greenery and seasonal interest.
- Street Trees & Planters:
  - Maintain existing street trees where feasible; where removal is required due to conflict with utilities or sight lines, replace with canopy trees consistent with City standards (e.g., willow oak or lacebark elm of at least 3-inch caliper at planting).
  - Incorporate large, at-grade planters with native or adaptive species such as dwarf yaupon holly, soft touch holly, black-eyed Susan, and ornamental grasses for year-round visual interest.
- Buffering and Screening:
  - Provide a low brick screen wall and evergreen shrub planting along the northern property line to buffer the adjacent residential use.
  - Screen the dumpster enclosure and any ground-mounted mechanical equipment with a combination of opaque fencing and evergreen plantings.

6. Signage and Lighting
- Signage:
  - Wall-mounted, externally illuminated signs above each ground-floor tenant entry, sized in accordance with the City’s sign ordinance and scaled appropriately to the façade.
  - Blade signs projecting over the sidewalk for pedestrian visibility, designed with metal brackets and panels that echo the building’s industrial-inspired aesthetic.
  - A modest, engraved metal plaque at the main lobby entrance identifying the upper-floor office tenants.
- Lighting:
  - Building-mounted, fully shielded LED fixtures will illuminate the primary entrances and sidewalk frontage without creating glare or light spill onto neighboring properties.
  - Accent lighting at the brick piers will provide subtle nighttime articulation of the façade, while remaining within recommended lighting levels for urban streetscapes.
  - Parking lot lighting will be provided by full-cutoff LED pole fixtures at a pedestrian-friendly height, with dimming controls for after-hours energy efficiency.

JUSTIFICATION AND RESPONSE TO GUIDELINES
1. Compatibility with Surrounding Context
The proposed design has been carefully developed to align with the scale, rhythm, and material character of nearby contributing buildings in the West End area. While the existing structure on site is not historically significant, the new building takes cues from the district’s established industrial and warehouse vernacular—particularly in its brick construction, vertically oriented fenestration, and clearly articulated base, middle, and top.

The three-story height is consistent with neighboring structures along River Street and strikes a balance between urban density and human-scale design. The step-back at the upper floors, along with the strong horizontal cornice line, helps to visually break down the massing and maintain an appropriate relationship to the sidewalk and pedestrian realm.

2. Pedestrian Orientation and Street Activation
The project strongly emphasizes ground-floor activation and pedestrian comfort. Storefronts are located directly on the sidewalk with large, transparent glazing that promotes visual connectivity between the interior and exterior. Entrances are oriented to River Street, with minimal interruption of the façade by service functions.

Outdoor seating opportunities have been incorporated in front of the southern retail bay, anticipating a potential café or restaurant tenant. This includes space for a small number of café tables, coordinated with the streetscape design to avoid obstructing pedestrian flow. These elements are intended to contribute to the vibrancy of the River Street corridor and support the City’s goals for an active, walkable downtown.

3. Material Quality and Durability
All primary façade materials are durable, high-quality products suitable for long-term use in an urban environment. Brick, precast concrete, and metal elements are designed to weather well and require minimal maintenance. The applicant has intentionally avoided highly reflective glass or overly contemporary finishes that would conflict with the established character of the West End.

Material transitions occur at logical locations such as inside corners or along structural lines, avoiding visually arbitrary changes. The color palette favors warm, earth-toned brick with dark, recessive metal accents, allowing the building to blend seamlessly into its context while still presenting a clearly contemporary expression.

4. Parking, Access, and Traffic Considerations
By locating parking behind the building and maintaining the River Street frontage as primarily pedestrian-oriented, the project supports the City’s design objectives for reduced visual dominance of parking areas. The number of on-site spaces is intentionally limited, given the availability of nearby public parking and the mixed-use nature of downtown, where walking and alternative transportation options are feasible.

The project team has reviewed preliminary access arrangements with a traffic engineer to ensure that vehicular movements into and out of the site will not create undue congestion or safety issues. The existing curb cut location is maintained to avoid new conflicts with adjacent intersections or crosswalks.

5. Sustainability and Resilience Measures
Although this application is not specifically for a green building certification, the design incorporates several sustainability-minded strategies, including:
- High-performance building envelope with enhanced insulation and energy-efficient glazing.
- LED lighting with occupancy sensors and daylight-responsive controls in common areas.
- EV-ready infrastructure in the parking area to support future electric vehicle charging.
- Native and drought-tolerant landscaping to reduce irrigation demand.
- Coordination with tenants to encourage bicycle use via secure indoor bike storage for office occupants.

6. Community and Economic Benefits
On a personal note, as the project architect and a downtown Greenville resident, I (Laura Kensington) have a vested interest in seeing River Street continue to evolve as a vibrant, pedestrian-oriented corridor. This project replaces a long-vacant, underutilized structure that has been the subject of repeated maintenance and code enforcement concerns with a thoughtfully designed building that can house local businesses and professional offices.

The anticipated tenant mix includes small, locally owned retailers and service providers rather than national chains. The building’s flexible floor plates are designed to accommodate start-ups and growing companies that want to remain in the downtown core but are often priced out of larger, Class A office towers. In discussions with the property owner, we have emphasized the importance of offering a range of suite sizes to support this goal.

REQUEST
In light of the above, we respectfully request that the Urban Design Panel grant a Certificate of Appropriateness for the proposed demolition of the existing non-contributing structure and construction of the new three-story mixed-use building as described. We believe the project is consistent with the City of Greenville’s design guidelines for the Downtown Design Overlay District, supports the ongoing revitalization of the West End, and will provide a long-term, contextually sensitive addition to River Street.

We welcome any conditions the Panel may deem appropriate regarding final material selections, lighting levels, signage details, or landscaping refinements, and we are fully prepared to work with staff to address any outstanding technical comments during the permitting phase.
